The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has updated its claim regarding a Taco Bell lettuce supplier and test results as Americans have been hit with illness.

The agency rechecked a lettuce sample and found the vegetable did not have traces of the parasite known as cyclospora, which can cause illness with symptoms including diarrhea, the New York Post reported Sunday.

“FDA is providing an update regarding the sample of lettuce supplied by Taylor Farms de Mexico which was reported positive on July 18, 2026,” the agency’s website reads:

Due to the complexity in detection of Cyclospora, FDA laboratory experts re-reviewed the sample results and have concluded that the finding does not represent true amplification and should be considered a false positive. Information about the sample has been removed from the July 18, 2026, update below. FDA has notified Taylor Farms and continues working with the firm to ensure product implicated in this outbreak has been removed from the market. FDA and state partners continue to collect and analyze product samples. As of July 19, 2026, there are no confirmed positive sample results for product testing for Cyclospora.

Also as of July 19, however, the FDA still advised, “Do not eat recalled iceberg lettuce from Taylor Farms de Mexico. FDA’s investigation is ongoing.”

Breitbart News initially reported Tuesday that Michigan health officials said lettuce or salad greens may be the source of an illness linked to “explosive” diarrhea hitting thousands of Americans.

Per the Post, the FDA apologized to Taylor Farms, the parent company of Taylor Farms de Mexico, regarding the false positive test result and admitted the mistake.

“The false alarm retracts the FDA’s Friday announcement, which linked the outbreak of the debilitating ‘explosive diarrhea’ parasite to the Taylor Fresh Farm’s subsidiary,” the Post article stated, adding officials initially said the contaminated lettuce was grown in Mexico before being transported to Taco Bell restaurants in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and West Virginia.

“Cyclosporiasis, the disease caused by the microscopic parasite Cyclospora, has ravaged the bowels of over 1,600 Americans in 34 states, according to the CDC,” the Post article said.

Meanwhile, some Democrats have tried to blame President Donald Trump for the illness outbreak, according to Breitbart News.

The lettuce was recalled in Alabama, Arkansas, Connecticut, Florida, Georgia, Iowa, Illinois, Indiana, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Massachusetts, Maryland, Michigan, Missouri, Mississippi, North Carolina, New Hampshire, New Jersey, Ohio, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas, Virginia, and Wisconsin, the FDA said.

centerText: Ann Widdecombe was hit on the head with a hammer 21 times at her home as she ate lunch in an attack that was caught on camera, prosecutors have told Westminster Magistrates' Court. Prosecutor Kashif Malik said the man charged with her murder, Joshua Kerry, arrived at her house on 8 July in a red Vauxhall Corsa and entered through the front door. Kerry, who prosecutors say was in the property for two minutes and was wearing black gloves, then allegedly hit Widdecombe repeatedly with a hammer in her kitchen, tipped her from her chair and took her wallet before driving off. The 28-year-old from South Yorkshire later also appeared at the Old Bailey and was remanded in custody. At the Old Bailey hearing, prosecutor Alistair Richardson said the attack on Widdecombe had been carried out with a "ferocity of violence". Malik told the court that Widdecombe did not attend a pre-arranged online interview with Channel 5's Matt Allwright show that had been scheduled for just before 13:00 BST on 8 July. The show's production team tried to contact Widdecombe through her personal assistant, who then asked Widdecombe's gardener to check on her, the court heard. Prosecutors said the gardener entered the house via the open front door on 9 July and found Widdecombe lying face down on her kitchen floor with a serious head injury.

centerText: The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz. The 10th consecutive night of strikes took place after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers. In response, Iran said it struck two oil tankers in the waterway, as well as US military assets in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, state news agency IRNA reported. President Masoud Pezeshkian said Iran was now fighting a "full-scale war" with the US. Trump earlier said Iran had been hit "very hard" the previous night "in honour" of three US soldiers killed recently - two in Jordan and one in Iraq. US Central Command (Centcom) said its latest strikes had hit Iranian "military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defence systems". Explosions were reported near the north-western city of Tabriz, where state media said one person was killed and several wounded, as well at the Gulf ports of Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini and the south-eastern ports of Chabahar and Konarak. Centcom also insisted that commercial vessels were continuing to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Iran meanwhile said its forces struck two oil tankers as they attempted to transit through what it called the strait's "unsafe and hazardous southern route" and rescue teams were picking up their crews, according to state media. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) said a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz had reported being struck by an unknown projectile, forcing its crew to abandon the vessel and board a lifeboat. It was not clear whether the tanker reported by UKMTO was among those cited by Iran. Six fuel tankers and cargo ships crossed the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, according to preliminary data from maritime intelligence firm Kpler. Two of these vessels had previously been sanctioned by the US for links with Iran. This is down from eight vessels crossing on 19 July, the data shows. All but one of the crossings made on 20 July were via the northern route near Iran. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) also claimed to have targeted a data centre belonging to the US company Amazon in Bahrain, according to state media on Tuesday. The IRGC "attacked and destroyed the central data infrastructure of the American company Amazon in Bahrain with several cruise missiles," state news agency IRNA reported. Jordan said on Tuesday that it had intercepted five drones after saying it had shot down three missiles late on Monday. Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected. Kuwait's armed forces also said they had intercepted Iranian drones. Later on Tuesday Kuwait said Iran had hit several power and water desalination plants the previous day. At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire. On Monday night the Department of Defense confirmed the identities of two of the US soldiers – Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, from Texas, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, from Hawaii - who were killed in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan over the weekend. Centcom said earlier a third service member also went missing in action after the attack, and unidentified remains were later found in the area.

WASHINGTON — Iran launched a fresh round of attacks on Kuwait Tuesday, escalating its campaign against US allies and partners in the Persian Gulf hours after American forces carried out a 10th consecutive night of strikes inside Iran.

Air raid sirens sounded across the emirate as Iranian missiles and drones targeted US-linked facilities and key infrastructure, regional sources told The Post. Kuwaiti authorities did not immediately release casualty figures or a full damage assessment.

“Kuwaiti Air Defenses are currently responding to hostile missile and drone threats,” the Kuwaiti Army said in a statement Tuesday morning. “The General Staff of the Army notes that if explosion sounds are heard, they are the result of the air defense systems intercepting the hostile attacks.”

topic: Trump Administration Sought Phone Records Times

left.title: Trump administration sought phone records of New York Times journalists and their relatives

left-center.title: Trump Administration Sought Phone Records of Times Journalists and Their Relatives

farLeftText: Donald Trump’s administration sought phone records of several New York Times journalists and even those of some of their relatives, according to a motion unsealed Monday – an unusually aggressive attempt to unmask the confidential sources of reporters who wrote about the president’s Air Force One jet from Qatar. The Trump administration’s push to compel the identity of sources in the form of additional subpoenas comes at a time when the executive branch’s relationship with the press is increasingly contentious. The efforts, described in a letter-motion filed by the Times over the weekend, were more expansive than previously known, the newspaper noted – and also covered a time frame that exceeded the news articles in question. “Two of the subpoenas seek records beginning on [1 January] 2026, long before the events that are purportedly the basis for the [justice] department’s investigation,” lawyers for the Times wrote in the letter. “That timeframe strongly suggests that the department is using this investigation not to focus on any purported concerns arising from the [8 and 9 July] articles, but instead to forage for information about the journalists’ source relationships more broadly.” The phone records subpoenas are on top of grand jury subpoenas served on 10 July on three journalists that sought to compel testimony from the reporters about the identify of their sources. The Times has separately challenged those grand jury subpoenas, and a US district judge, Arun Subramanian, is scheduled to hear arguments on Thursday. The judge has also frozen enforcement of the phone records subpoenas pending a ruling on the newspaper’s motion to quash those. Lawyers for the newspaper noted that some of the phone records subpoenas were issued on the same day as the grand jury ones, an additional one was issued on 14 July, and another was issued on 16 July – after the Times had already moved to quash the grand jury subpoenas. In arguing for the phone records subpoenas to be spiked, the Times said the government had acted in “bad faith” and had ignored its own protocols by not giving the journalists advance notice that records were being sought and by demanding the information “without first conducting any serious investigation”. The newspaper’s lawyers said the additional subpoenas call “into question the accuracy” of testimony given recently by Jay Clayton, the US attorney in Manhattan, where the grand jury subpoenas were issued. On Wednesday at his confirmation hearing to be the Trump administration’s director of national intelligence, Clayton said that “we followed the protocols” and that the government had taken “the least intrusive possible” steps. The justice department has justified the grand jury subpoenas by saying that “reporters are not the targets, those leaking classified information are”. The additional subpoenas included a request for phone records of one reporter’s mother and two of the journalists’ spouses. The motion noted that the mother in question is a mental health professional with confidential client relationships and that one of the two spouses is the general counsel of a law firm. “The disclosure of additional subpoenas for confidential newsgathering information of the journalists is deeply concerning, revealing yet another instance of the alarming pattern of conduct detailed in the motion to quash,” the news organization’s lawyers wrote. “These actions demonstrate abuse of the grand jury process, continued bad faith attacks on the journalists, violations of the department’s own internal regulations and disregard for the law in this circuit intended to protect critical first amendment interests,” the motion stated, alluding to the part of the US constitution that addresses press freedom. In a response posted on Tuesday morning, the justice department said that it had complied with its own regulations in issuing the subpoenas and that it had taken investigative steps before seeking them. The department said it was authorized to delay disclosure of the subpoenas but had decided to alert lawyers for the journalists to give them an opportunity to challenge. Get in touch Contact us about this story The best public interest journalism relies on first-hand accounts from people in the know. If you have something to share on this subject, you can contact us confidentially using the following methods: Secure Messaging in the Guardian app The Guardian app has a tool to send tips about stories. Messages are end to end encrypted and concealed within the routine activity that every Guardian mobile app performs. This prevents an observer from knowing that you are communicating with us at all, let alone what is being said. If you don’t already have the Guardian app, download it (iOS/Android) and go to the menu. Select ‘Secure Messaging’. SecureDrop If you can safely use the tor network without being observed or monitored you can send messages and documents to the Guardian via our SecureDrop platform. Our guide at theguardian.com/tips lists several ways to contact us securely, and discusses the pros and cons of each. Show more The grand jury subpoenas, delivered to reporters at their homes, marked a dramatic escalation of the Trump administration’s crackdown on media leaks that free press advocates swiftly condemned as a government effort to intimidate news organizations. It followed an FBI search in January of a Washington Post reporter’s home and the seizure of her electronic devices. The new jet in question, a present from Qatar that Trump’s administration spent $400m to retrofit and upgrade, recently entered service. But Trump used an older model Air Force One jet to leave a Nato summit in Turkey earlier in July. The Times, citing anonymous sources, reported that the switch had come at the urging of the US Secret Service – and that the newer plane lacked some of the advanced security features of the older Air Force One aircraft, including antimissile capabilities. On social media, Trump denied security concerns.

topic: Andy Burnham Becomes Britains Ahead World

left.title: ‘A big job ahead of him’: world leaders congratulate Andy Burnham as he becomes Britain’s PM

center.title: News Wrap: Andy Burnham becomes Britain's 7th prime minister in a decade

farLeftText: World leaders have warmly congratulated Andy Burnham on his entry into 10 Downing Street as Britain’s seventh prime minister in a decade, but international media largely focused on the scale of the challenges awaiting him. Emmanuel Macron, France’s president, said he hoped for a deeper relationship “in the service of our peoples, and by giving new momentum to the partnership between the UK and the EU” rekindled by Burnham’s predecessor, Keir Starmer. The German chancellor, Friedrich Merz, who spoke with Burnham on Monday evening including about continued support for Ukraine, said Germany and the UK were close partners and he looked forward to working together. The president of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, said she looked forward to “strengthening the EU-UK partnership”. A “very good first discussion” had revealed a common commitment to “bringing the EU and UK closer and closer”, she later added. Beyond the diplomatic platitudes, however, media coverage focused on Burnham’s regional persona – and the huge task awaiting him. France’s Public Sénat said the new prime minister came from “the working class of the north of England”. He had managed to “build an image as an ‘everyman’, appearing without a tie, very often in a T-shirt”, the political TV channel said on its website, standing out as “a politician on the ground, close to everyday concerns”. Italy’s Corriere della Sera said the UK was experiencing “profound unease, instability and social resentment” and “needs a push” forward, but Burnham’s nationalisation, state intervention and reindustrialisation plans suggested he saw the past as a model. The UK “appears incapable of imagining and planning for the future and prefers to take refuge in a backward-looking Eden”, it argued, even though the pre-Thatcher years were marked by “energy rationing, rubbish in the streets and IMF bailouts”. 8:31 Andy Burnham becomes Britain’s new prime minister - The Latest The Irish Times noted that Burnham may be “a breath of fresh air” but the challenges that brought down Starmer – “a budget unable to meet desperately needed demand in defence, housing, welfare, care for the aged, and training for the young” – remained. The new prime minister, the paper said, would be “hamstrung by a commitment to abide by strict fiscal rules. His instinct may be to tack to the left, but he is already trying to prove that … he will govern from the centre” and the job was “immense”. In an New York Times essay titled The King of the North Won’t Save Britain, Tom Hazeldine said that with his “northern accent, casual attire and everyman persona”, Burnham “trades on his outsider status, a precious commodity in antipolitical times”. But, Hazeldine added, a UK that had wanted “a bounce” after Starmer had actually ended up with – in the absence of anything better – “a career Blairite with a northern soul and a Whitehall brain. For the country, it looks a lot like more of the same.” Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, wished Burnham “a successful tenure”, adding that Italy and the UK were “bound by a deep friendship and a collaboration that, over the years, has grown ever stronger” and she was sure would continue. Outside the EU, Donald Trump, who had previously described Burnham as “the mayor of a town” and “extremely liberal”, said on Monday night the two had had a “very good conversation” on topics including North Sea oil. “The Prime Minister has got a big job ahead of him, but he will be able to do it and, of course, the U.S.A. will be there to help!” Trump posted on Truth Social. “The call was interesting, and went very well. I wished Prime Minister Burnham, GOOD LUCK AND GODSPEED!” Ukraine’s president, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, described the UK as “one of the global leaders”, adding that the two countries’ relationship was “stronger than ever and Kyiv stood “ready to work together in all possible formats”. Anthony Albanese, the Australian prime minister, said Australia and the UK were “longstanding friends” bound by “deep ties”. He looked forward to strengthening the partnership and promoting “security, resilience and prosperity in the years ahead”.

centerText: In our news wrap Monday, Andy Burnham became Britain's seventh prime minister in a decade, Andrew and Tristan Tate will remain in federal custody as they fight extradition to the UK where they face dozens of new rape and sex trafficking charges and protests in New Delhi turned violent after police intervened in a student-led demonstration calling for reforms to India's education system. Notice: Transcripts are machine and human generated and lightly edited for accuracy. They may contain errors. Amna Nawaz: We start the day's other headlines in the U.K., where Andy Burnham became Britain's seventh prime minister in a decade, vowing to tackle a range of issues from the cost of living to health care. He first traveled to Buckingham Palace, where King Charles formally asked him to form a government. (Applause) Amna Nawaz: Burnham then enjoyed a warm welcome at his new official residence of 10 Downing Street. The former mayor of greater Manchester replaces Keir Starmer, who resigned amid pressure from his own party. In his first remarks, Burnham pledged what he called a circuit breaker moment to change the course of Britain's politics. Andy Burnham, British Prime Minister: Let's build a new national sense of unity, of common purpose and positivity. Let us make this the moment when Britain starts to believe again, the moment we bring back hope. Amna Nawaz: Burnham is set to announce parts of a 10-year agenda tomorrow, as he appoints new members to his Cabinet. President Trump was among the world leaders congratulating Burnham on his new role. The controversial influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate will remain in federal custody as they fight extradition to England. The brothers were arrested in Miami this weekend and face dozens of new rape and sex trafficking charges in the U.K. Speaking to reporters, their lawyer insisted the brothers are innocent and called the allegations against them politically motivated and -- quote -- "garbage." The dual U.S. and U.K. citizens are prominent figures in the so-called manosphere, where they promote their own brand of hypermasculinity and misogynistic views. The judge set another court hearing for next Monday. In New York City, the FBI says a former U.S. Army soldier set off an incendiary device outside a federal building that houses a number of government offices, including an immigration court. Eyewitness video shows a man pouring a liquid onto a set of steps before setting it ablaze. The individual was quickly wrestled to the ground, and authorities identified him as 43-year-old Andrew Arrabaca. Investigators say they found what appeared to be anti-ICE material in his possession, including a cart that read "ICE Off Our Streets." Authorities say he also came, in their words, armed with bad intentions. James Barnacle, Assistant FBI Director in Charge: Today, the extremism was brought here. The building was attacked. Mr. Arrabaca was looking to injure people and harm people. He had two axes, a hammer, a machete, and three knives. Amna Nawaz: Three people suffered minor injuries in the attack. No charges have been announced, and the investigation is ongoing. Protests in New Delhi turned violent today after police intervened in a student-led demonstration calling for reforms to India's education system. Tear gas canisters boomed through the streets, and police beat people with batons. Some protesters threw stones at security officers dressed in riot gear. The chaos erupted as supporters of the so-called Cockroach Movement marched toward Parliament, demanding that the education minister resign amid anger over the leak of a national exam. Today's protests were among the largest against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government in years. In Mississippi, family, friends, and supporters paid tribute at a funeral today for Nolan Wells and demanded answers about the death of the Black teenager who went missing after a July 4 boat trip with his white friends. Christine Wonsley, Mother of Nolan Wells: Nolan had such a gentle, calming spirit about him. Amna Nawaz: His mother, Christine Wonsley, remembered the 18-year-old as a gifted athlete and a role model to his younger siblings. Reverend Al Sharpton gave a eulogy in which he criticized what he called the unanswered questions in the teen's death. And he invoked the history of racist violence in Mississippi, which included the killings of Emmett Till and Medgar Evers. Rev. Al Sharpton, Civil Rights Activist: Based on the history, we got to be able to dig deep. We can't have people in law enforcement saying there was no foul play before an investigation. Amna Nawaz: Sharpton promised a $50,000 reward for new information about the case, an amount later matched by actor and producer Tyler Perry. An investigation is ongoing. President Trump signed orders today that would impose 50 percent tariffs on a wide range of Canadian goods, including wine, cement and hockey sticks. The White House says it's using a section of the Tariff Act of 1930 to justify its levies, saying they're in response to what it calls Canada's discriminatory treatment of American products. It's just the latest threat to the economic relationship between the U.S. and its second largest trading partner. The new tariffs are set to take effect in 30 days. In the meantime, on Wall Street today, stocks drifted lower to start the week. The Dow Jones industrial average fell around 300 points. The Nasdaq slipped just 12 points, so almost flat. The S&P 500 also closed with a small loss. And Vice President J.D. Vance and second lady Usha Vance have welcomed their fourth child, a son named Alec Neel Vance. President Trump posted this photo on social media earlier today, offering his congratulations to the Vance family. The baby is the first child born to a sitting vice president in more than 150 years. In a statement, the vice president wrote -- quote -- "Usha and the baby are happy and healthy, and our kids are overjoyed to meet their little brother." The 41-year-old Vance has often advocated for Americans to have more children. We offer our congratulations to the family as well.

topic: Russian Warship Exercise Coast Carries Rare

left.title: Russian warship carries out rare live-fire exercise 45 miles off UK coast

center.title: Russian warship carried out firing exercise off Plymouth coast

farLeftText: A Russian warship fired its main cannon in a short notice test 45 miles south of Plymouth on Monday, the day Andy Burnham was appointed as prime minister. The Neustrashimy suddenly announced it intended to fire its guns in the morning and requested that a British warship, which had been tracking it, retreat a couple of miles to a safe distance. The patrol vessel HMS Tyne repositioned five miles away, allowing the Russian frigate to conduct a 30-minute firing drill at about 9.30am in international waters to the south of the UK. Wes Streeting, on his first full day as UK defence secretary, described the incident as “performative and irresponsible”. “It’s the tip of the iceberg of the daily threats that this country faces and our allies face.” Russian warships have been sailing around British waters for much of 2026. Last month another frigate, the Admiral Grigorovich, fired warning shots within a few hundred metres of a couple on a British pleasure yacht, south of the Isle of Wight. Conducting a live-fire exercise at short notice in international waters close to another country is a rare event and usually conducted with due notice. However, if proper safety warnings are given it is legal. The 130-metre-long Neustrashimy is armed with a 100mm cannon capable of firing 50 rounds a minute at a range of 20km. It is also armed with anti-ship cruise missiles and has Gatling rotary machine guns as part of its air defence systems. British defence sources said they could not remember the last time a Russian warship had test-fired so close to the UK. Firing exercises are usually conducted with particular care in the Channel, one of the world’s busiest commercial waterways. The Neustrashimy arrived off the British coast earlier this month, replacing the Admiral Grigorovich which had been on an extended three-month deployment, typically in the Channel or the North Sea. On Tuesday morning the Neustrashimy was sailing east of the Isle of Wight, and thought likely to be heading into the North Sea, where it may refuel. It is now being tracked HMS Mersey, which has taken over shadowing responsibility from HMS Tyne. Maritime tensions between the UK and Russia are in a heightened state. A Russia-linked oil tanker was seized in the Channel by the Royal Marines last month, prompting warnings that there could be a Russian retaliation. Russian warships have been used to escort some oil tankers, though at other times their presence is designed to be psychological and tie up UK naval resources in monitoring them. Dmitry Peskov, the Kremlin’s spokesperson, said Russian warships always acted “in strict accordance” with international and maritime law. Asked whether the Russian government felt relations with the UK would improve under the new prime minister, Peskov said it had “no such hopes”. A Ministry of Defence spokesperson said: “A Russian naval vessel shadowed by the Royal Navy conducted a live-fire weapons exercise in international waters approximately 40 nautical miles south of Plymouth yesterday. “The Royal Navy monitored that exercise throughout, continues to track the vessel’s activity closely, and stands ready to protect UK national security.”

centerText:
The incident will be a reminder for the new prime minister of increased Russian naval activity near UK waters in recent months. UK defence sources would not comment on whether the incident might have been timed to coincide with the start of Andy Burnham's premiership.

Last month, Royal Marine Commandos boarded a Russian "shadow fleet" oil tanker in the English Channel in a six-hour operation which was the first of its kind by UK armed forces.

Also in June, a British couple in international waters in the English Channel came across a Russian warship - Admiral Grigorovich - 23 miles off the Isle of Wight.

The frigate fired warning shots as Jane and Alan Kelvey sailed their yacht on what Russia's defence ministry said had been a "dangerous approach". However, the couple said they had not been on a "collision course".

Russian warships regularly pass through international waters in the Channel, which are separate from UK and French territorial waters.

BBC Verify has analysed flight and ship-tracking data which shows the position of HMS Tyne and several British military aircraft operating in and around the Channel near Plymouth on Monday.

Flight data on ADS-B Exchange indicates at least four Royal Air Force and Royal Navy aircraft were flying off the coast of Dorset mostly above the UK's territorial waters, including two Eurofighter Typhoons, a Voyager air refuelling tanker and a Super Lynx helicopter.

A French military H160M Guépard helicopter also flew out and over the Channel during the afternoon. It is not clear if this is the aircraft which made contact with the Russian warship.

Data on ship-tracking service MarineTraffic shows HMS Tyne began to broadcast its location about 40 miles off Plymouth at 13:57 BST on Monday. It remained in that location for around two hours before departing at 15:56 to return to Plymouth.

HMS Tyne has been tracking Neustrashimy since at least 16 July when both vessels were also captured in the North Sea about 36 miles miles from the port of Felixstowe in Suffolk on a satellite image.

Additional reporting by Ella Kipling, Barbara Metzler, Thomas Spencer and Patrick Jackson

left.title: What the EU wants from Andy Burnham

right-center.title: Andy Burnham Is Bad News for British Business

farLeftText: Andy Burnham is unknown in Brussels, but that will soon change. In his schedule for the autumn is an EU-UK summit that may underscore the same dilemmas that have confronted previous British prime ministers in the relationship with Europe. EU leaders were quick to offer congratulations to Britain’s prime minister, the seventh since the 2016 Brexit referendum. The European Commission president, Ursula von der Leyen, said she looked forward to strengthening the EU-UK partnership “for the security of our shared continent” and “the prosperity of people on both sides of the Channel”. The German chancellor, Friedrich Merz, invited Burnham to visit Berlin “very soon”, noting that the UK and Germany’s “deep connection is particularly evident in these times”. Georg Riekeles, a former adviser on the commission’s Brexit taskforce, told the Guardian: “People don’t know what to expect. But he also starts with a blank sheet of paper … and that can be an advantage.” The main concern, said Riekeles, who is now an associate director at the European Policy Centre, was whether Burnham would be “on the same trajectory” in terms of defence and security cooperation, energy and the “very positive agenda” on economic issues. One EU diplomat observed: “For a British prime minister it is not necessarily a bad thing to be little known. If you are well known it is probably because you were prominent on one side or the other of the Brexit debate.” Burnham’s most notable statement on the EU was his wish to see Britain rejoin in his lifetime, while speaking to the Guardian at the Labour party conference in September 2025. But during campaigning in the leave-voting constituency of Makerfield, he made it clear reversing Brexit was not a priority, because “Britain would be stuck in a permanent rut if we are just constantly arguing”. Michael Jacobs, who advised the former prime minister Gordon Brown, said Burnham’s statement on rejoining “felt like it was from the heart” but was also politically astute. “It tells pro-Europeans that he is one of them. But it tells leave voters that it’s not his political priority for now, and he is not going to relitigate the Brexit arguments.” Among the first calls Burnham had after entering No 10 were with von der Leyen, Merz and the French leader, Emmanuel Macron. He has appointed Hamish Falconer as the Europe minister, replacing Nick Thomas-Symonds. The latter’s departure is a blow to the UK-EU reset talks started by Starmer, but some claimed they had lost momentum and Falconer would bring fresh energy. Elected as an MP in 2024, Falconer brings a wealth of international experience, with a decade spent in the Foreign and Commonwealth Office, a stint in the National Crime Agency, and a career dealing with international terrorism, hostage recovery and countering human trafficking. He served as Middle East minister under Starmer. The return of Johnny Reynolds, as business secretary, will help in talks aimed at protecting European trade from China, and tricky negotiations to scrap an upcoming 10% tariff on exports of British cars to the EU and vice versa. Under Starmer, the UK and EU signed a security and defence pact and cooperated closely on Ukraine. Despite failing to agree British participation in a €150bn EU defence loans scheme, the two sides moved on. In Starmer’s final week in office the UK signed an agreement to participate in the EU’s €90bn (£77bn) loan for Ukraine, meaning British companies can provide more weapons funded through the scheme. Now Burnham inherits the reset agenda launched by Starmer. The two sides are due to sign a trio of agreements at the summit: a deal to reduce paperwork and certification for fresh food in an effort to ease trade barriers for British exporters; linking emissions trading systems; and a youth exchange scheme enabling 18- to 30-year-olds to work, study, travel and volunteer in the EU and UK. Ireland’s Europe minister, Thomas Byrne, told reporters this month that he expected “positive continuity, at the very least”. Ireland, which holds the EU Council’s rotating presidency until the end of the year, will be involved in the diplomatic preparations for the summit, the date of which is yet to be decided. “There are tricky issues there, and I think we can get them over the line,” Byrne said. Talks have been deadlocked over the youth mobility agreement. EU member states think the UK’s proposed cap on numbers is too low and they oppose their nationals paying the NHS surcharge and higher rate of tuition fees for foreign students. But Some diplomats see a middle ground whereby EU students get a discount on foreign fees but pay above the domestic rate. Jacobs suggested Burnham may raise the cap on numbers. “The idea that leave voters regard such a scheme as a form of immigration was always slightly absurd, and I think Burnham will see that as a relatively easy win to offer pro-Europeans.” But when it comes to the UK-EU economic relationship, Burnham will face the same constraints as Starmer. Earlier this year the EU rejected an attempt by Starmer to negotiate a single market for goods, minus the free movement of people. EU member states suggested the UK could join the European Economic Area, but that would require accepting the free movement of people and making payments to the EU budget with no voting rights in shaping new EU rules. Jacobs said: “Given Burnham’s sensitivity to the immigration concerns of his own Makerfield voters, and of many others, the idea that he would seek to re-enter the single market by allowing free movement and making a massive financial payment to the EU budget (which is what the EU would demand) seems implausible. “On the substantive issues, I think he will do pretty much what Starmer has been trying to do with the so-called ‘reset’, and he will come up against the same barriers in Brussels.”
